title: Subarray Sum Equals K
code: public static int SubarraySum(int[] nums, int k)
var count = new Dictionary<long, int> { [0] = 1 };  // empty prefix
result += seen;                              // subarrays ending here that sum to k

frames.caption: Seed count[0] = 1 (the empty prefix), sum = 0, result = 0. Then sweep left to right.
frames.note: The empty prefix has total 0 and must be in the map before the loop. Without count[0] = 1 you would miss every subarray that starts at index 0 — for those, the matching "earlier prefix" is the one before any element. Seeding it once removes the special case so the same lookup handles ranges starting anywhere.

frames.caption: i=0: x=1. Extend the running prefix: sum += 1 → sum = 1.
frames.note: sum is the prefix total of nums[0..i] — every element seen so far, including this one. We never materialize a prefix array; one scalar carries the running sum, which is why the whole algorithm is O(1) extra work per element on top of the map.

frames.caption: Look up sum − k = 1 − 3 = -2. count[-2] = 0 (no earlier prefix equals -2) → result unchanged at 0.
frames.note: The identity is prefix[j+1] - prefix[i] = k, rearranged to prefix[i] = sum - k. So "how many subarrays ending here sum to k?" becomes "how many earlier prefixes equal sum - k?" — and the map answers that in O(1). We look up BEFORE recording the current prefix so we only ever pair with a strictly earlier index, never with this position itself. The seed count[0] = 1 stands for the empty prefix, which is what lets subarrays starting at index 0 be counted.

frames.caption: Record this prefix: count[1] = 0 + 1 = 1.
frames.note: Only after the lookup do we increment count[sum]: this prefix value now becomes a candidate left-endpoint for every element that comes after it. Counts (not lengths) are what we accumulate — the same prefix value can recur, and each recurrence is a distinct subarray, so result can grow by more than one in a single step. This "look up, then record" order is the heart of the one-pass O(n) technique.

frames.caption: i=1: x=2. Extend the running prefix: sum += 2 → sum = 3.
frames.note: sum is the prefix total of nums[0..i] — every element seen so far, including this one. We never materialize a prefix array; one scalar carries the running sum, which is why the whole algorithm is O(1) extra work per element on top of the map.

frames.caption: Look up sum − k = 3 − 3 = 0. Found count[0] = 1 → result += 1 → result = 1.
frames.note: The identity is prefix[j+1] - prefix[i] = k, rearranged to prefix[i] = sum - k. So "how many subarrays ending here sum to k?" becomes "how many earlier prefixes equal sum - k?" — and the map answers that in O(1). The seed count[0] = 1 stands for the empty prefix, which is what lets subarrays starting at index 0 be counted.

frames.note: Only after the lookup do we increment count[sum]: this prefix value now becomes a candidate left-endpoint for every element that comes after it. This "look up, then record" order is the heart of the one-pass O(n) technique.

frames.caption: i=4: x=1. Extend the running prefix: sum += 1 → sum = 7.

frames.caption: Look up sum − k = 7 − 3 = 4. Found count[4] = 1 → result += 1 → result = 4.
frames.note: The identity is prefix[j+1] - prefix[i] = k, rearranged to prefix[i] = sum - k. So "how many subarrays ending here sum to k?" becomes "how many earlier prefixes equal sum - k?" — and the map answers that in O(1). frames.caption: Record this prefix: count[7] = 0 + 1 = 1.
frames.note: Only after the lookup do we increment count[sum]: this prefix value now becomes a candidate left-endpoint for every element that comes after it. Counts (not lengths) are what we accumulate — the same prefix value can recur, and each recurrence is a distinct subarray, so result can grow by more than one in a single step. frames.caption: Loop ends: 4 subarrays sum to 3 — [1,2], [2,1], [1,2], [2,1]. Return result = 4.
frames.note: Every step asked one O(1) question — "how many earlier prefixes equal sum − k?" — and summed the answers, so the total is O(n) time and O(n) space for the map. Crucially this counts overlapping subarrays and handles negatives, zeros, and positives alike: it compares prefix VALUES by equality, making no monotonicity assumption, which is exactly why a sliding window would be unsound here.
